text.content:
Beta Note: The Monster Starter Pack was rewritten to better reflect the current state of DC20 Core Rules development. Every section has been updated to align with the Beta 0.10.5 game statistics. This document will be the foundation of Combat Encounter building going forward. The Game Master Tips section has been removed as that content is better suited for the Game Master Guide section of the DC20 Core Rules.

Creating exciting, fair, and memorable battles is at the heart of DC20. This document provides tools and guidelines for building monsters and designing combat encounters that challenge your players without overwhelming them. You’ll learn how to balance enemy strength, damage output, action economy, encounter difficulty, and how many enemies overall.

These are the core design philosophies for a standard DC20 Combat:

  • Accuracy: Attacks have a 65% chance to hit targets.

  • Balance: A Monster is about as strong as the same level PC

  • Quantity: There are the same amount of monsters fighting the same number of PCs.

  • Difficulty: Combat Encounters are designed for Medium difficulty.

  • Duration: Combat Encounters last an average of 3 Rounds.

DC Tip: Hard difficulty monsters are designed to be killed by PCs of the same level in 3 Rounds to ensure the design goal of 3 Round Combats is met.

Example: If an average Combat Encounter lasts 3 Rounds, a PC makes 1-2 Attacks per Round, and the PC has a 65% chance to hit the monster with an Attack, then the PC would make 4-6 Attacks across the 3 Rounds and hit the monster with 3-4 Attacks. If the monster needs to be defeated in those 3 Rounds, then the Attacks each need to deal 25%-33% of the monster’s max HP to defeat it.

Building a monster in DC20 means fine-tuning a few key statistics to strike the right balance between challenge and fun. By adjusting these numbers, GMs can design encounters that stay exciting and fair, keeping players engaged without making the fight too easy or too punishing.

To begin designing a monster, use the statistics below as a foundation. These values represent the baseline for an average monster at each level, providing a balanced starting point before applying role-specific adjustments or unique Traits (see Monster Traits section for more information).

LevelAverage HPAverage PD/ADAttack BonusSave DCPrime ModifierCombat MasteryTrait Value

A monster’s offensive power defines its role in combat and shapes how players experience the encounter. Whether it’s a brutal melee bruiser, a cunning ambusher, or an arcane threat that controls the battlefield, its damage output and attack design determine how dangerous it feels. Balancing a monster’s offense ensures that every encounter is tense and exciting without becoming unfair or overwhelming. The following guidelines will help you set appropriate damage values, design balanced attacks, and fine-tune how your monsters challenge the party.

Since Combat Encounters are designed to last 3 Rounds, a monster’s Damage per Round should be based on the health of a PC of an equal level and the desired monster difficulty. The table below lists the average amount of damage a monster should deal per Attack, organized by level and difficulty. Use these numbers as a baseline when designing or adjusting monster attacks. When designing a monster of a particular level, the Hard column of the table is used primarily.

LevelEasyMediumHardVery HardDeadly
Novice0.250.250.51.52
00.250.5122.5
10.250.5123
20.511.534
30.5123.55
411.52.546
511.52.54.56.5
61.5235.57.5
71.523.55.58
81.52.5469
91.52.54710
10235811
1123.558.512
122.545.5913
132.5469.514
1434.56.51014.5
1534.56.510.515.5
163.55711.516
173.557.51217
1845.5812.518
1945.581318.5
204.56913.520

Low Level Damage: The damage value of an Attack can be less than 1 at early levels - especially on Easy difficulty.

Half Increments: When the damage value is divisible by 0.5, the Attack has the Impact Property.

Examples:

  • 1.5 = Attacks deal 1 damage and have Impact.
  • 0.5 = Monster has 2 AP and Attacks deal 1 damage.
  • 0.25 = Monster has 2 AP and Attacks deal 0 damage and have Impact.

When designing a monster’s Attacks, consider how many creatures each Attack can target, how much damage it deals, and how many Action Points (AP) it should cost. These guidelines help ensure that monsters feel balanced, dynamic, and appropriate for the difficulty.

  • Single-Target Attacks: An Attack that targets only 1 creature should cost 1 AP.
  • Multi-Target Attacks: An Attack that targets 2 or more creatures (such as an Area Attack) should cost 2 AP. You can adjust this based on the size and reach of the Area of Effect.

Example: A typical Level 1 PC has about 9 HP, so a Level 1 monster in a Hard difficulty Combat Encounter should deal about 2 Damage per Round. If it makes 2 single-target Attacks per Round, each should cost 1 AP and deal 1 damage. If it makes 1 Attack that targets 2 creatures, the Attack should cost 2 AP and deal 1 damage.

A monster’s survivability determines how long it can stay active in combat and how much effort the party must invest to defeat it. In DC20, survivability is shaped primarily by Health Points, Defenses, and Resistances. Balancing these elements ensures your monsters feel threatening without dragging out the fight or making the encounter frustrating. Each stat plays a distinct role in defining how survivable a creature is on the battlefield.

Health represents a monster’s raw endurance. The more HP it has, the longer it can withstand attacks before being defeated. Increasing HP makes a monster more reliable over time, since it can continue to take hits even when facing multiple attackers or high-damage characters. However, giving a monster too much Health can make battles feel slow or repetitive, so it is often better to pair moderate HP with other defensive tools like Resistance or crowdcontrol abilities.

A monster’s Precision Defense (PD) and Area Defense (AD) determine how hard it is to hit.

  • Precision Defense (PD): Protects against attacks that are made against a specific target.
  • Area Defense (AD): Protects against wide-reaching attacks that cover an area, usually containing multiple targets

Raising these Defenses reduces how often the monster gets hit by attacks rather than how much damage it takes. High Defenses work well for well protected or highly evasive creatures, but too much avoidance can make encounters feel unfair or slow if the party struggles to land hits

Resistances reduce the amount of damage a monster takes after being hit. They represent natural toughness, armor, or magical wards that blunt the impact of attacks. Granting Resistance is an effective way to make a monster feel resilient without relying solely on high HP. For example, an armored knight might resist physical damage, while an elemental spirit resists fire or cold.

DC Tip: True damage can’t be reduced or mitigated in any way, so Resistance doesn’t work against it. It’s recommended to avoid giving monsters abilities that deal True damage.

Damage Reduction is a special form of Resistance that only reduces damage from normal Hits. It doesn’t protect against Heavy Hits or Critical Hits, which bypass this protection. Against normal Hits, DR functions the same as Resistance, reducing the monster’s incoming damage by a set amount or percentage. This makes DR a useful tool for monsters that should shrug off light attacks but remain vulnerable to strong, well-placed strikes

DC Tip: Physical Damage Reduction (PDR) works against Bludgeoning, Piercing, and Slashing damage. Elemental Damage Reduction (EDR) works against Cold, Corrosion, Fire, Lightning, and Poison damage. Mystical Damage Reduction (MDR) works against Psychic, Radiant, and Umbral damage.

A monster’s Speed determines how far it can move each time it takes the Move Action (1 AP). In DC20, most monsters are designedwith a Speed of 5,which matchesthe default Speed of PCs. This makes encounters predictable and balanced, allowing both players and GMs to plan movement and positioning around similar rates of motion.

Speed should vary for monsters that are naturally faster or slower than humanoid characters. Creatures known for agility, flight, orswiftness might have higher Speed to reflect their mobility, while slower, lumbering monsters should have lower Speed to emphasize their cumbersome nature.

Fast Monsters: These monsters can harass ranged or weaker characters, reposition frequently, or threaten multiple areas of the battlefield. A warhorse might have a Speed of 8, allowing it to charge across the battlefield and engage or disengage quickly.

Slow Monsters: These monsters often rely on durability, area control, or powerful attacks to compensate for their limited mobility. A turtle or golem might have a Speed of 2, moving deliberately and requiring strategic positioning to become a threat.
Most monsters have 4 Action Points (AP). They use AP during their Turn to take Actions, or during another creature’s Turn to take Reactions if the situation allows.

How a monster spends its Action Points defines its combat style, level of threat, and overall impact on the battlefield. A creature that focuses all its AP on a flurry of attacks can quickly overwhelm a wounded PC, while one that mixes movement and strikes can control the battlefield and pressure multiple targets at once.

  • Heavy Attack: Empower a single, devastating attack (Attack Action + Enhancements).
  • Multi-Attack: Make multiple attacks in a single turn (repeated Attack Action).
  • Move & Attack: Move and strike to gain positional advantage (Move Action + Attack Action).
  • Signature Ability: Trigger special abilities, defenses, or support effects (Spell Action or Feature).

Thoughtful AP use raises the tension of combat and pushes players to adapt their tactics, manage their resources wisely, and coordinate as a team to overcome the monster’s shifting strategies.

Monsters can be granted Traits to give them unique strengths, weaknesses, and special abilities. These Traits help define a monster’sidentity, making encounters distinct, memorable, and strategically engaging. By assigning at least one Trait per monster, you create opportunities for players to adapt their strategies and face a variety of challenges. Traits can highlight a monster’s role in combat, its personality, or its connection to the environment, ensuring that every encounter feels different.

DC Tip: Certain Traits can increase or decrease a monster’s damage, HP, or Defenses. It is highly recommended that you not grant a monster more than 2 of any kind of these Traits.

Example: A shadowy creature might have a Trait that makes it difficult to detect but vulnerable to light-based attacks (such as the Light Spell or Radiant damage).

When referencing the Monster Statistics Table to determine the appropriate Trait value for a monster at a given level. The number shown represents the total value provided by that monster’s Traits. Each Trait a monster possesses (whether offensive, defensive, or utility-based) contributes to this total. A single strong Trait might account for the full value, while several minor Traits could combine to reach it.

Determining Trait Value

The value is based on how much bonus AP it provides per 3 Round Combat (1 Trait Point = 2 AP).

DC Tip: Certain Traits can increase or decrease a monster’s damage, HP, or Defenses. It is highly recommended that you not grant a monster more than 2 of any kind of these Traits.

Monsters can also gain Class Features or versions of them, giving them traits typically associated with PCs. For instance, a monster could have a Champion’s Adaptive Tactics, a Cleric’s Channel Divinity, or a Rogue’s Cheap Shot, allowing for more dynamic and versatile encounters. A Class Feature is worth roughly 2 Trait value for a monster

Example: A Goblin with a Rogue’s Cunning Action and Cheap Shot can strike quickly and exploit vulnerabilities in the party’s defenses (4 total Trait value). A Giant with a Barbarian’s Rage Feature can deliver devastating attacks that test the party’s endurance and planning (2 total Trait value).

DC Tip: Using Class Features on monsters is a great way to showcase abilities from Classes that are not represented by the party’s PCs, giving players a sense of the wider possibilities within the game.

Monster roles describe how a creature can be used most effectively in combat. They help GMs select monsters that work well together and fulfill different functions during a battle. These roles do not provide mechanical bonuses or penalties by themselves, but they give insight into how a monster was designed and how it might behave in combat alongside others.

The table below outlines the typical stat adjustments used to tailor a monster to a specific monster role.

RoleHP ModifierDamage ModifierDefense ModifierTrait ValueTypes of Traits
Brute+25%+25%-4---Resistances, Self Buffs
Defender+25%---+2---Crowd Control, Debuffs
Leader----25%---+4Auras, Help, Repositioning
Soldier------------Basic Combat Benefits
Striker-25%+50%-1---Ranged Attacks, Mobility
Tactician-25%-25%---+8Crowd/Zone Control, Healing

DC Tip: Add or subtract a monster’s Well-Bloodied value to increase or decrease its HP by 25%. To adjust its damage by 25% you can add or subtract 0.5 damage per the following levels: Easy (every 5 Levels), Medium (every 4 Levels), Hard (every 3 Levels), Very Hard (every 2 Levels), and Deadly (every 1.5 Levels).

Designing a well-balanced encounter is one of the most rewarding parts of running DC20. A good encounter should challenge the party without overwhelming them, encourage tactical decision-making, and keep the tension high. This system uses a level-based approach that closely aligns monster levels with the levels of the player characters (PCs), helping Game Masters (GMs) create fair and exciting battles.

When creating a Combat Encounter, the total number of monster levels should generally match the total number of PC levels in the party. This creates a balanced, Medium difficulty Combat Encounter where both sides are near evenly matched but the party is favored to win. This guideline ensures the party faces a fair challenge without excessive risk of a total party defeat, unless luck or strategy swings heavily in one direction

Example: A party of 4 x Level 3 PCs would have an Encounter Budget of 12 (4 x 3 = 12).

Once you know your party’s total level, compare it to the combined level of the monsters to determine the difficulty of the encounter. You can increase or decrease difficulty of a Combat Encounter by adjusting the damage dealt by the monsters (see the Monster Damage section) or by increasing or decreasing their level or quantity as show in the table below.

DifficultyTotal Monster Level
DeadlyEncounter Budget + 4 x Average PC Level
Very HardEncounter Budget + 2 x Average PC Level
HardEncounter Budget + Average PC Level
MediumEncounter Budget
EasyEncounter Budget - Average PC Level

• Deadly: These Combat Encounters are designed to be truly life-threatening. A frontal assault is rarely wise; survival often depends on retreat, clever tactics, or exploiting a narrow advantage.
  • Very Hard: Victory demands sharp tactics, coordinated teamwork, and careful positioning. One mistake can shift the fight, and players must actively work together to turn the odds
  • Hard: Clever parties can seize victory through smart play, resource management, teamwork, coordination, and sharp tactics
  • Medium: The party holds a modest advantage. The threat is real enough to keep players alert, but they are expected to prevail unless they take major risks.
  • Easy: These encounters should feel safe while remaining interesting. The party can showcase their abilities without much danger, making them good warmups or moments of momentum.

The simplest way to balance an Encounter is by ensuring there are an equal number of Monsters and PCs that are of the same level. However, you can convert a Monster into multiple Monsters of lower levels, provided their total levels add up to the same value.

Example: You could convert a Level 6 Monster into 2 x Level 3 Monsters or 3 x Level 2 Monsters.

DC Tip: Monster level conversion doesn’t work as well with Monsters that are Level 3 or lower. This is because a Level 2 Monster is only ~60% as strong as 2 x Level 1 Monsters (when you add their HP and damage together), so when you convert a Level 2 Monster into 2 x Level 1 Monsters you increase the danger by +75% (the danger should ideally remain the same). When using Monster level conversion on Level 2 Monsters, budget the Encounter for 1 Difficulty lower than normal. For example, you could convert 1 x Level 2 Monster of Hard difficulty to 2 x Level 1 Monsters of Medium difficulty.

The action economy is one of the key elements that determines the difficulty of a combat encounter. It represents the total number of Actions a side can take in a single Round, measured by their combined Action Points (AP) The relationship between the monsters’ total AP and the party’s total AP dictates how frequently each side can act, attack, reposition, and react to changing conditions on the battlefield.

If you want to increase or decrease the number of monsters without changing the encounter’s difficulty, adjust their available AP so that the total monster AP remains roughly equal to the total PC AP. This ensures both sides have comparable opportunities to act each Round, keeping the flow of combat dynamic and fair

Epic and Legendary monsters gain special options that expand their tactical reach: Round Actions and Reaction Points.

Beta Note: Round Actions were formerly called Epic Actions, and Reaction Points were formerly called Legendary Action Points (LAP). They were both renamed to distinguish them from Epic and Legendary monsters respectively, and to make it clearer what function they have in the game.

Round Actions make large battles feel epic and ensure powerful creatures maintain presence and pressure throughout a fight. A monster with Round Actions can automatically perform the stated Action at any time during the stated Round, unless the monster is Incapacitated. These Actions may be the same each Round or unique to each Round, depending on design intent. Each Round Action should be roughly equivalent to a 2 AP Action the monster could take normally.

Reaction Points allow a monster to act more frequently outside of its own Turn, keeping the fight dynamic and unpredictable.

Using Reaction Points

A monster can spend Reaction Points to take Actions, Reactions, or perform AP enhancements in the following ways:

Reactions: The monster can spend Reaction Points to take Reactions available to them.

Example: If the monster has the ability to perform a Spell Duel, they could spend 2 AP to challenge a Spell cast by another creature. Alternatively, they could spend 2 Reaction Points instead of 2 Action Points to perform the Spell Duel.

Actions (End of PC Turn): At the end of a PC Turn, the monster can spend Reaction Points instead of Action Points to take an Action. If an effect prevents you from taking Reactions, you can still spend Reaction Points to take Actions at the end of a PC Turn. If multiple PCs share the same PC Turn, the monster can take a number of Actions up to the number of PCs that occupy that PC Turn

Example: If 2 PCs are sharing a Turn (see Shared Initiative in the Core Rules), when their Turn ends, a monster with Reaction Points can take 2 Actions (1 Action per PC). They could take the Attack Action twice, take the Move Action then the Attack Action, or another other 2 Actions of their choice.

AP Enhancements: When the monster spends Reaction Points to take an Action or Reaction, they can spend additional Reaction Points to perform AP Enhancements with that Action or Reaction. The total amount of Action Points and Reactions Points spent on an Action or Reaction can’t exceed 4.

Example: A PC just ended their Turn. The monster can spend 1 Reaction Point to take the Attack Action and make an Attack against a target within range. The monster can add any AP Enhancements to the Attack, spending an amount of Reaction Points equal to the AP cost required. The AP cost of the Attack plus the Enhancements can’t exceed 4 AP, because the monster can’t spend more than 4 Reaction Points per Action.

Regaining Reaction Points

The monster regains all spent Reaction Points at the end of its Turn.
The monster regains all spent Reaction Points at the end of its Turn.
